title: OSPF DR/BDR Election
description: Election of Designated Router and Backup DR on multi-access networks to reduce OSPF adjacency overhead.
fullExplanation: On multi-access networks (Ethernet), if every OSPF router formed a full adjacency with every other router, the number of adjacencies would be n(n-1)/2 — unscalable. The DR (Designated Router) and BDR (Backup Designated Router) solve this: all routers form full adjacency only with the DR and BDR. The DR generates Type 2 Network LSAs and manages LSDB synchronization for the segment. The BDR monitors the DR and takes over immediately if it fails.

electionProcess.description: Routers send Hello packets and wait for the dead interval to expire
Router with highest OSPF priority becomes DR (default priority = 1)
Router with second-highest priority becomes BDR
Tie-break: highest Router ID wins (highest loopback or interface IP)
All other routers become DROther — form Full adjacency only with DR and BDR
DROther routers stay in 2-Way state with each other

multicastAddresses.name: AllSPFRouters
multicastAddresses.description: All OSPF routers listen here. DROthers send LSUs to this address.

multicastAddresses.name: AllDRouters
multicastAddresses.description: Only DR and BDR listen here. DROthers send updates to DR/BDR via this address.

keyFacts: Priority 0: router will never become DR or BDR
Election is non-preemptive: existing DR keeps role even if higher-priority router joins
To force re-election: clear ip ospf process (resets all OSPF adjacencies)
Point-to-point links: no DR/BDR election needed — only 2 routers
DR failure: BDR immediately becomes DR, new BDR election held
DR/BDR are per-segment, not per-router — a router can be DR on one segment and DROther on another
